title: What the dishes look like — and how to order

items.name: Samgyeopsal · 삼겹살
items.body: Thick pork-belly strips you grill at the table, wrapped in lettuce with garlic, ssamjang and kimchi. Order by the portion (인분, in-bun) — say "삼겹살 2인분" for two.

img.alt: Pork belly grilling on a tabletop grill with onion

items.name: Bibimbap · 비빔밥
items.body: Rice topped with seasoned vegetables, egg and red chili paste — mix it all together before eating. Point and say "비빔밥 하나" (one bibimbap). Dolsot (돌솥) means the sizzling stone bowl.

img.alt: Bibimbap with arranged vegetables and gochujang over rice

items.name: Tteokbokki · 떡볶이
items.body: Cylindrical rice cakes simmered in a sweet-spicy gochujang sauce — the classic street snack. Grab it from any pojangmacha (street stall) or a bunsik shop.

img.alt: Tteokbokki rice cakes in red sauce with sesame seeds

items.name: Chimaek · 치맥
items.body: Korean fried chicken with beer — the national evening ritual. "반반" (banban) gets you half plain-crispy, half sweet-spicy yangnyeom, the safe first order.

img.alt: Half crispy, half seasoned Korean fried chicken with pickled radish

items.name: Naengmyeon · 냉면
items.body: Cold buckwheat noodles in an icy broth (mul, 물) or with spicy sauce (bibim, 비빔) — exactly what July heat calls for. Snip them with the table scissors before eating.

img.file: KOCIS Mul-naengmyeon, Chilled Buckwheat Noodle Soup (4594756202).jpg
img.alt: Cold naengmyeon noodles in a metal bowl with ice, egg and beef

items.name: Gamjatang · 감자탕
items.body: A rich, spicy pork-spine-and-potato stew that bubbles at the table — hearty late-night or hangover food. Ordered by pot size for the group (소/중/대 = small/medium/large).

img.alt: Gamjatang pork spine stew with potato in red broth

items.name: Hotteok · 호떡
items.body: A chewy pancake stuffed with molten brown sugar, honey and cinnamon, pressed on a street griddle — winter-market perfect, sold year-round. Just point and hand over ₩1,000–2,000 (⚠ price varies by stall).

img.alt: Hotteok sweet pancakes cooking on a street griddle

items.name: Bingsu · 빙수
items.body: Fluffy shaved milk-ice piled with sweet red bean (pat, 팥), fruit and condensed milk — the café heat-beater. Patbingsu (팥빙수) is the classic; share one, they're big.

img.alt: Bowls of Korean bingsu shaved ice with red bean and fruit

title: What to eat
body:
Hit the obvious greats: Korean BBQ, chimaek (fried chicken and beer), tteokbokki, and market street food at Gwangjang — bindaetteok (mung-bean pancake), mayak gimbap, and live octopus if you're brave.

You're based in Jongno — old-Seoul heart, with Insadong, Ikseon-dong and Gwangjang Market a short walk away plus walk-in BBQ and café culture. Central for eating well without tourist-strip prices. For markets: Gwangjang Market (~5 min, Line 1 east 2 stops) and Namdaemun (Line 1 → Seoul Station, ~10 min). Myeongdong (K-beauty, street stalls) is a ~12-min walk — worth a half-day. Yongsan for beef and electronics (Line 1 south, ~10 min). In July heat, naengmyeon (cold buckwheat noodles) and bingsu (shaved-ice dessert) are exactly what you want. Café culture is everywhere when you need air-conditioning.

title: Where locals review restaurants — apps and platforms
body:
Google Maps ratings are thin in Korea — locals use different platforms entirely. These are what Koreans actually open before eating out:

  • Naver Map (네이버 지도) — the dominant platform. Search a restaurant, tap the listing and you'll see star ratings, visitor photos, and linked blog reviews (블로그 후기). The blog posts are often detailed and photo-heavy — even without Korean, the photos show whether the food looks right and whether the space is clean. In English mode, star ratings and photos are readable; use Papago for any text. This is the first check for anything in central Seoul or Hongdae. Download the app before the trip.
  • Kakao Map (카카오맵) — integrated with the Kakao T taxi app, so a restaurant search can go directly to navigation or hailing a cab. Ratings are Korean-first but photos are universal.
  • Daytripkorea — English-friendly café and eatery finder with editorial curation; good for vetted shortlists rather than raw listings.

The practical flow: Search the place name on Naver Map first — below 4.0 on 1,000+ reviews, skip it; good photos and recent blog posts, trust it. If the listing looks thin, search the same name on Kakao Map before writing the place off — the two don't always carry the same listings or the same reviews. ⚠ No single app is authoritative here: check any place that matters against a second source, and treat a place with no app presence as unknown rather than bad.

title: Ordering in Korean restaurants — the mechanics
body:
More accessible than it looks from the street. The mechanics that make it easy:

  • Kiosks — most chain restaurants and many AYCE BBQ spots have self-ordering touchscreens. Tap the flag icon for English, then browse photo menus and add items. No conversation. At AYCE BBQ kiosks also handle your meat refill orders during the meal.
  • Picture menus — most mid-range sit-down restaurants have laminated picture menus or a QR code that opens photos. Point at what you want, hold up fingers for quantity.
  • Papago camera — for text-only menus, open Papago, tap the camera icon, and point at the page. It overlays live Korean-to-English translation without you typing anything.
  • Walk in and sit — if a table is open, take it. A server will come. Most casual Korean restaurants don't seat you formally. Hold up fingers to show group size if asked.
  • Banchan is automatic and free — you'll receive a set of small side dishes (kimchi, egg rolls, salads) without ordering them. Always free, always refillable, always included. Don't order them or pay for them separately.
  • Call button — most Korean restaurants have a button on the table to summon a server. Press it to order more or ask for the bill. If there isn't one, yeogi-yo! (여기요) gets attention — completely normal, not rude.
  • Paying — walk up to the register when you're done. Bills are rarely brought to the table. Card is accepted almost everywhere; some market stalls are cash-only.
  • The phrase that handles most orders: point at the menu and say igeo juseyo (이거 주세요 — this one, please).

What to order beyond BBQ — the overlooked ones:

  • Jjigae (stew) — doenjang jjigae (fermented soybean), kimchi jjigae, or sundubu jjigae (silken tofu). Served boiling in a stone pot, ≈₩8,000–12,000 (⚠ varies by venue), usually comes with rice and banchan. Available everywhere; good breakfast or recovery food after a long day.
  • Dolsot bibimbap — rice and vegetables in a hot stone bowl with a raw egg that cooks on contact; crispy rice forms at the bottom. Mix everything together before eating. Safe, filling, ≈₩12,000–15,000 (⚠ varies by venue).
  • Somaek — the default way to drink in Korea: pour a shot of soju into a glass of beer and drink together. Every table at every chicken or pork spot does it. No special ordering needed — just ask for soju and beer.

title: How to book restaurants in Korea
body:
Korea's restaurant booking landscape is divided: popular spots require reservations; most neighbourhood BBQ joints and street food stalls are walk-in only. Here's how to book, in order of convenience for foreign visitors.

Catch Table Global — the primary tool for foreigners
Korea's dominant reservation platform now has an international version at catchtable.net (search 'Catchtable' on the App Store). No Korean phone number required, accepts Visa/Mastercard/Amex, available in English, 8,000+ restaurants listed. Deposits are standard (typically ₩10,000–30,000 per person, deducted from the bill or refunded after your visit). In-demand KBBQ restaurants, omakase spots, and Michelin-listed venues use Catch Table almost exclusively. Download before you leave.

Naver Map reservations — directly from the map
When you search a restaurant on Naver Map and see a blue 예약 (reservation) button on the listing, you can book directly in the app. As of June 2026, Naver now accepts passport-based identity verification for foreign visitors — no Korean phone number needed. Most popular mid-range spots use it; confirmation can be instant or take up to a day.

Phone — for smaller neighbourhood places
Some local restaurants take calls only. Most won't answer in English — ask your Airbnb host to call, or message through the restaurant's Naver/Kakao Map listing (many respond faster to a chat message than a call).

Walk-in reality check for this trip: Neighbourhood BBQ joints, ramen counters, and market stalls are walk-in only. For the specific restaurants in this guide: Ungteori Meats now recommends booking via Catch Table — weekday lunch is the most reliable walk-in window; weekend evenings queue. MUSSO and Chicken Baengi are reliably walk-in. Gobang Sikdang in Daejeon should be called on weekends (042-863-2104). If you're set on a specific spot for a meaningful night, book it before you land.

title: Where to eat — picks near your route
body:
Two clusters: near-base picks in Jongno for everyday meals, and Hongdae picks (near Hongik University Station, Line 2) for your nights out there. All prices ≈ — verify before you go.

Near the Jongno base: walk-in Korean BBQ, kalguksu and bunsik line Jongno and Insadong 5–10 min away, and the Gwangjang Market food alleys (below) are two Line-1 stops east. For the arrival-night dinner, any Jongno samgyeopsal joint does the job.

Hongdae street food — the pedestrian zone and Wausan-ro strip. Any evening you're over there. No booking. Tteokbokki, hotteok, skewers, grilled corn — ≈₩2,000–5,000 per item.

AYCE Korean BBQ — budget pork belly (arrival-night pick):

  • Ungteori Meats (엉터리생고기) — 118 Eoulmadang-ro, Mapo-gu. From Hongik Univ. Station: ~10 min walk. Open 11:00–23:30 daily. ≈₩14,800 pp; confirm on Creatrip. Fresh (not frozen) samgyeopsal. A Hongdae-night pick (from the Jongno base, save it for the Jul 10 raid evening). Reserve on Catch Table Global for weekend evenings — weekday lunch is the most reliable walk-in window. Naver Map.

AYCE Korean BBQ — mid-range / beef option:

  • MUSSO Hongdae (무쏘 홍대입구역점) — 131 Eoulmadang-ro (지층/basement), Mapo-gu; Hongik Univ. Station Exit 8, ~2-min walk. Open ≈12:00–01:00 daily — not 24 hr (⚠ confirm same-day for a late visit). ≈₩20,800–23,800 pp (beef; shrimp and yukhoe at the higher tier). Walk-in. Good for a Jul 14 Hongdae send-off dinner or a late bite after MSI. Naver Map.

KBBQ backups — if Ungteori or MUSSO are full:

  • Palsaik Samgyupsal (팔색삼겹살) — the 8-flavour pork belly concept: same cut marinated in eight sauces (garlic, herb, ginseng, curry, mushroom, red wine, soybean paste, original) served together. Multiple Hongdae branches; bookable on Klook if you want to pre-pay. Walk-in usually possible early evening weekdays. ≈₩13,000–16,000 pp ⚠ — confirm at venue. Naver Map.
  • Saemaul Sikdang (새마을식당 홍대점) — 144 Eoulmadang-ro, Mapo-gu. Popular chain known for staff-assisted grilling; the server manages the fire, so it's the most hands-off KBBQ option. Server brings water kimchi broth as a palate cleanser between cuts. ≈₩13,000–17,000 pp ⚠. Walk-in. Naver Map.
  • GIT TTEUL (깃들) — small neighbourhood BBQ on Jandari-ro 3an-gil (the indie bar and café alley running parallel to the main pedestrian zone). Authentic, locally-frequented, moderate prices, less tourist-facing than the main strip. Walk-in. ≈₩15,000–20,000 pp ⚠. Naver Map.

Chimaek (fried chicken + beer):

  • Chicken Baengi (치킨뱅이 홍대점) — 127 Eoulmadang-ro, Mapo-gu. From Hongik Univ. Station: ~10 min walk. Mon–Thu 16:00–01:00; Fri–Sun 14:00–01:00 ⚠. ≈₩10,000–20,000 pp; confirm at venue. Garlic chicken is the standout. Walk-in; busy Fri/Sat after 20:00 — arrive early. Best on Day 5 (Sun Jul 12) if the Daejeon pair returns early. Naver Map.

Cold noodles (July heat):

  • Yookssam Naengmyeon (육쌈냉면 홍대2호점) — 35 Wausan-ro 23-gil (2F), Mapo-gu; near Hongik Univ. Station Exit 9, ~5-min walk. Open ≈11:00–22:30 (⚠ one listing shows 10:30–21:00; confirm on yookssam.com). Naengmyeon set with charcoal-grilled bulgogi; ≈₩15,000–18,000 pp ⚠. Walk-in for lunch; dinner can queue. Naver Map.

Gwangjang Market (광장시장) — 88 Changgyeonggung-ro, Jongno-gu. From the Jongno base: Line 1 east 2 stops to Jongno 5-ga, Exit 7–8 (~5 min) — the market's on your doorstep now. Food vendors from 09:00; eating alleys open most days — ⚠ individual stall hours vary. Cash only at most stalls. Bindaetteok ≈₩4,000, mayak gimbap ≈₩3,000. A weekday is emptier; it fits as the Monday fallback if you skip Busan (see the Monday contingencies), or as a lunch stop near Insadong on Tue Jul 14 after the Secret Garden. Walk-in only.

Bingsu:

  • Sulbing (설빙) — find the nearest Hongdae branch; confirm on sulbing.com. Open ~12:00–24:00. Injeolmi bingsu ≈₩8,900; mango/melon ≈₩14,900–15,900 ⚠. No booking. Perfect mid-afternoon on any hot day.

Daejeon — near DCC II (Expo district, Yuseong-gu):

  • Sungsimdang DCC Branch (성심당 DCC점) — 107 Expo-ro, Yuseong-gu, same street as DCC II, short walk. Open daily 08:00–22:00. Legendary Daejeon bakery; fried soboro and chive breads. Walk-in; grab breakfast before the arena on Sat Jul 11. Naver Map.
  • Ossi Kalguksu Doryong (오씨칼국수 도룡점) — Doryong-dong 413, Yuseong-gu, ~0.2 mi from DCC II. Highly-rated knife-cut noodle soup (the Doryong branch and the Samseong-dong original are the genuine ones — ignore copycats). Open ≈11:00–21:00 — ⚠ weekend hours unconfirmed; call ahead (042-861-9972). ≈₩9,000–12,000 pp. Walk-in. Naver Map.
  • Cham-i-mat Gamjatang (참이맛감자탕&순대국 대전엑스포점) — 엑스포로151번길 19 (same building as Gobang Sikdang, different floor), ~5 min walk from I-Hotel. Open 11:00–22:00 daily (last order 21:30; Sun split: 11:00–15:00 & 17:00–22:00). Pork-bone stew and blood-sausage soup — hearty recovery food. ≈₩9,000–13,000 pp. Walk-in. Naver Map.
  • Gobang Sikdang Expo Branch (고반식당 대전엑스포점) — 엑스포로151번길 19 (2F), ~5–10 min walk from I-Hotel. Mon–Fri 16:00–24:00; Sat–Sun 12:00–23:00. Premium aged Korean pork grilled by staff. ≈₩20,000–30,000 pp ⚠. Book ahead weekends: 042-863-2104. Naver Map. Best: aged-pork celebration night.
  • Yakiniku Doryong (야끼니꾸 도룡) — 엑스포로151번길 19, 1F (same building as Gobang), ~5–10 min from I-Hotel. Hanwoo yakiniku-style grill — ox-tongue (우설), 한우 모듬, yukhoe; a year-end hanwoo set ≈₩50,000 pp ⚠. Mon–Sat 17:00–23:00 · closed Sundays. The beef pick after Saturday's LBF (Gobang next door is aged pork). Search '야끼니꾸 도룡' on Naver Map.

Post-match dinner in Daejeon — pick by how late it runs. The Grand Final (Sun, 15:00 start) likely ends 18:00–20:30; the Lower Bracket Final (Sat, 17:00 start) can run to ~22:00.

  • For beef: Yakiniku Doryong (hanwoo, 1F of Gobang's building, Mon–Sat to 23:00 — closed Sun) is the Saturday pick; for Sunday beef try Hanmaeum Jeongyuk Sikdang (한마음정육식당 도룡점 — butcher-shop beef near DCC; ⚠ confirm Sun hours) or Saebyeok-so (새벽소, Dunsan — latest-open hanwoo, ~15–20 min taxi).
  • For pork / wrapped by ~21:00: Gobang Sikdang (aged pork; book ahead), Cham-i-mat (gamjatang, last order 21:30), or Ossi Kalguksu if you arrive before 21:00.
  • 21:00–23:00: Gobang Sikdang is the safest sit-down (kitchen to 24:00 Mon–Fri / 23:00 Sat–Sun); Cham-i-mat only if you make the 21:30 last order.
  • After ~23:00, or for a night out: Dunsan-dong (Seo-gu, ~15–20 min taxi) is Daejeon's nightlife strip — 24-hour budae-jjigae, chimaek and makgeolli bars (search '둔산동' on Naver Map for what's open now). For chimaek closer to the hotel, the BBQ / Kyochon / BHC chains have Yuseong branches open to ≈01:00 — check Naver Map day-of. Always-open fallback: the GS25 / CU and pojangmacha tents around the I-Hotel.

Jul 10 lunch — near Gyeongbokgung (Gwanghwamun area):

  • Gwanghwamun Mijin (광화문미진) — 종로구 종로 19 (르메이에르종로타운 1F). From Gyeongbokgung main gate: ~10 min walk south toward Gwanghwamun Square. Open 10:30–21:00 daily (last order 20:30). 2026 Michelin Bib Gourmand. Cold buckwheat noodles (냉메밀) and warm versions; ≈₩12,000/bowl. Walk-in; lunch queue 12:00–13:00 — arrive before noon or at 13:30. Best fit: right after Gyeongbokgung, before the Bukchon afternoon.

Jul 14 lunch — near Anguk / Changdeokgung:

  • Bukchon Sonmandu (북촌손만두) — 종로구 관훈동 42-2, near Insadong. From Changdeokgung gate: Anguk Station Exit 6, ~10-min walk south. Open 11:00–20:30 daily. Three-generation mandu specialist: fried dumplings (튀김만두), steamed dumplings, manduguk. ≈₩3,500/serving, cold noodles ≈₩5,500. Walk-in only. Best fit: after the 10:30 Huwon tour, before Insadong shopping.

Convenience store — the underrated late-night move:

  • GS25 and CU everywhere, open 24 h: triangle gimbap ~₩1,500, instant ramen ~₩1,500, egg salad sandwich ~₩2,500, tteokbokki cups. After a full GO Fest / MSI day, often the right call.

title: Breakfast near the base
body:
Korea doesn't do big hotel-style breakfasts, but Jongno has good options from 07:00–07:30:

  • Isaac Toast — the toasted-egg-sandwich chain has branches around Jongno/Insadong; look for the yellow signs. Opens ~07:00. Korean-style toasted egg sandwiches ~₩3,500–5,500 (⚠ chain pricing, confirm at the register); 5–10 min queue on weekdays, longer on weekends. Quick, cheap, no language needed — point at the menu pictures. The best fast breakfast before an early palace day.
  • Paris Baguette / Tous les Jours — both chains have branches within a few minutes of the base. Opens ~08:00. Coffee, croissants, egg sandwiches, sweet pastries — reliable, air-conditioned, and fast. Good on days when you're heading somewhere early.
  • Cafés in Ikseon-dong / Insadong — the hanok lanes a few minutes east are dense with independent cafés that open from 08:00 (some earlier on weekends). Most serve egg-toast, bagels, or light breakfast sets alongside coffee. A block off the main street is cheaper and often very good.
  • Convenience store (GS25 / CU) — everywhere, open 24 h. Triangle gimbap ~₩1,500, egg-salad sandwich ~₩2,500, hot coffee from machine ~₩600. The pragmatic call on early departure days, post-late-night mornings, or any day you want to eat at the base. GS25 and CU are within a 1-min walk anywhere in Jongno.

title: Late-night food after midnight
body:
Seoul's late-night food scene runs well past midnight. In Hongdae — where you'll be for the raid and send-off nights — it's right outside the door.

  • Hongdae restaurant strip — most restaurants in Hongdae run until 04:00, some 24 hours. After clubs: ramen shops, pojangmacha tents (street-food stalls), and simya sikdang (late-night diners serving comfort Korean food from 22:00 to sunrise). Mapo-gu's designated pojangmacha zone near Hongdae concentrates the best street tents legally. No transit, no planning — just walk out and find it.
  • MUSSO Hongdae — open until ≈01:00 (not 24 hr; listed in the picks section above); works as a late AYCE option up to last call. ~2-min walk from Hongik Univ. Station Exit 8. After ~01:00, the convenience stores and pojangmacha tents take over.
  • Boseung Hoegwan (보승회관 명동점) — 13 Myeongdong 9-gil, Jung-gu, Seoul. Open 24 hours. The standby for post-midnight hangover soup (haejang-guk): pork rib soup, ramen, and white sliced pork. ≈₩10,000–20,000 per person (⚠ verify on-site). 5-min walk from Euljiro 1-ga Station Exit 6. Worth the ~30-min trip from Hongdae if you specifically want haejang-guk; otherwise the Hongdae strip has you covered.
  • Convenience store (GS25 / CU) — always the reliable backup: instant ramen at a hot-water station, tteokbokki cups, kimbap, snacks. No closing time.

title: Shopping game plan — low-hassle route
body:
The idea: most of the load is gifts for other people (K-beauty, K-pop). So buy the heavy for-others stuff last and nearest the airport, keep everything else central and hands-free, and let tax refunds + bag storage do the carrying. Three rules:

  1. Bulk K-beauty on departure morning — Olive Young Jonggak, across from the base → straight into checked bags or the airport luggage courier → VAT refund at ICN. You never carry a skincare haul through the July heat.
  2. Use in-store tax refund + bag storage. Olive Young Myeongdong Town knocks the ~10% VAT off at the register (passport out) and holds your bags so you shop hands-free.
  3. Stay central and covered. The Jongno–Insadong–Myeongdong core is walkable and underground-connected — good in a downpour or mid-30s heat. Only Stüssy needs a river crossing, folded into Tuesday.

By day:

  • Fri Jul 10 — esports, no detour. LoL Park Riot store is at your base (Jonggak Exit 1); T1 Base Camp shop (Hongdae, 10:30–22:00) folds into that evening's raid night. Both merch stops happen where you already are.
  • Tue Jul 14 — the main run. After the 10:30 Secret Garden, a noon Apgujeong dash (Line 3 Anguk→Apgujeong) for Stüssy + Gentle Monster + Kasina cafe lunch; buy and drop the bag, back central by ~15:00. Then Myeongdong: Olive Young flagship (K-beauty gifts, tax refund + bag storage), Line Friends Square and the K-pop street. Ends a short hop from A Flower Blossom on the Rice — your booked send-off dinner.
  • Wed Jul 15 — the bulk haul, then go. Slow morning at Olive Young Jonggak for the big gift load → courier / checked bags → 6002 → VAT at ICN.

K-pop stores — corrected: HYBE Insight (the Yongsan museum) has closed — HYBE merch is pop-ups now, check the Weverse Shop the week before. SMTOWN COEX Artium closed in 2020; SM's store is now KWANGYA@Seoul in Seongsu (Ttukseom, Line 2; ~10:30–20:00) — a separate trip, or skip it for the central Myeongdong K-pop options.

Store quick-reference (⚠ confirm hours before you go):

  • Olive Young Myeongdong Town — ~10:00–22:30; 2 floors, in-store VAT refund, bag storage.
  • Olive Young Jonggak — across from the base; the departure-morning haul.
  • Stüssy Seoul Chapter — 42 Apgujeong-ro 46-gil, Gangnam; 11:00–20:00; Kasina cafe upstairs.
  • LoL Park Riot store — Gran Seoul, Jonggak Exit 1; ~10:00–19:00.
  • T1 Base Camp — 147 Yanghwa-ro, Hongdae (B1); shop 10:30–22:00.
  • Line Friends Square — Myeongdong; ~11:00–22:00.
  • KWANGYA@Seoul (SM) — Seongsu, Ttukseom Exit 5; ~10:30–20:00.

VAT refund: spend ≥₩15,000 at a tax-free shop and show your passport for the instant discount, or claim the stamped receipts at the ICN departures kiosk before check-in.

title: Shopping in Seoul
body:
You're based in Jongno — Insadong crafts, Gwangjang Market and LoL Park on your doorstep. By district from Jonggak (Line 1): Insadong — 5–10 min walk (crafts, souvenirs); Myeongdong — ~12 min walk or Line 1 to Euljiro for K-beauty (Olive Young flagship, Innisfree); Dongdaemun — Line 1 east ~8 min (late-night shopping to 04:00); Gangnam / COEX — ~30 min (Line 1 to City Hall → Line 2) for malls (SM's KWANGYA store is in Seongsu — see the shopping plan). Tax-free shops offer a VAT refund on purchases over ₩15,000 — instant in-store or at the airport kiosk on departure.

Streetwear in Hongdae (a Line-2 hop from central Seoul): Musinsa Standard flagship (144 Yanghwa-ro, Mapo-gu; open 11:00–21:00; near Exit 9) — Korean casualwear at a wide price range. The alley cluster around Wausan-ro (Mahagrid, SOUR, indie labels) rewards a slow browse — 10–15 min walk from Hongik Univ. Station. Gentle Monster Hongdae (54 Dokmak-ro 7-gil; open 12:00–22:00) is an art-installation eyewear store — free to walk through. Ader Error has moved to Seongsu (Seongdong-gu, Line 2 ~30 min); worth the trip if design-forward Korean fashion is your thing.

Esports, anime & gaming: LoL Park (now CHZZK LoL Park) — the official LCK arena at Gran Seoul, 33 Jong-ro, Jongno-gu; Jonggak Station (Line 1) Exit 1. The Riot Store, café and exhibits are open to walk-ins ≈10:00–19:00 (arena seating needs an LCK match ticket); the in-house PC bang runs 24 h. Visit Seoul listing. Animate Hongdae — Japan's anime-goods chain on 5F of AK Plaza (188 Yanghwa-ro), right at Hongik Univ. Station Exit 4; open 11:00–21:40; figures, manga, game and VTuber merch. Yongsan i'Park Mall (Yongsan Station, ~10 min via Line 1 south) stacks electronics, trading cards and figures across several floors.

title: Souvenirs — Korea
items: K-beauty from Olive Young (sheet masks, skincare)
Korean snacks (honey-butter chips, Pepero, Market O)
Kakao Friends / Line Friends character goods
A celadon or craft piece from Insadong

title: Collectibles & chain merch
body: For collectors: a Starbucks 'Been There' Seoul mug, and the Kakao Friends and Line Friends flagship stores (Hongdae and Gangnam) for character-brand exclusives you won't find at home.

title: Quick reference — when you're unsure what to do
body:
Open this when no one can agree on a plan.

  • Hungry, near the base: any Jongno/Insadong BBQ walk-in, or the Gwangjang Market alleys (2 stops east). In Hongdae for the night? Yookssam Naengmyeon or Chicken Baengi.
  • Want KBBQ tonight: Walk the side streets off Wausan-ro after 17:00 — a dozen AYCE spots, no reservations needed mid-week. Budget ≈₩15,000–25,000 pp.
  • Fastest possible meal: GS25 or CU — triangle gimbap (≈₩1,500), corn dog, instant ramen at the counter. Genuinely good and a Seoul experience.
  • Hot and need AC: any café, the Jonggak/Jongno underground arcades, or COEX Mall (Samseong, Line 2). Free to enter, browse as long as you want.
  • Nothing to do in the afternoon: Han River — Line 5 to Yeouido Station, buy fried chicken from the strip at Exit 3–4, sit on the grass by the water. Free, always worth it.
  • Want to raid but don't want to sightsee: Jongno, Insadong and Cheonggyecheon are gym-dense near the base; Hongdae and Sinchon for a bigger cluster. Drop a Lure at a café table and grind.
  • Evening with no plan: the Ikseon-dong and Insadong lanes on your doorstep, or ride to Hongdae's pedestrian zone — buskers, street food, energy toward midnight.
  • After midnight: MUSSO (AYCE Korean BBQ, open until ≈01:00) or any GS25 with ramen — after ~01:00 it's the convenience stores and pojangmacha tents. Mike's Cabin if someone wants a bar.
  • Daejeon: gap between events: Hanbat Arboretum (free, walkable from EXPO, great PokéStop density) or the EXPO Science Park grounds. Both have shade.

title: 2026 Tax Refund Optimization
checklist: Passport out at every checkout — in-store immediate refund requires it (no passport = no same-day refund)
₩15,000 minimum per receipt to qualify; split a large purchase if needed to get separate qualifying receipts
Olive Young: split purchases across 3–4 transactions in ₩100,000 increments — each qualifies independently for the refund counter
Lotte Mart / large supermarkets (e.g. the Seoul Station branch): free boxes + tape at the exit — staff will pack snacks and groceries for the flight at no cost
ICN Terminal 1: tax-refund kiosk in the departures level before security — bring all stamped receipts; refund issued as cash or card credit on the spot
